critic_raw:
## Analysis

This is a reasonably well-designed experiment. The preprocessing discipline (fit on train only), single test-set evaluation, stratified splits, tuning parity, and explicit scope limitations are all appropriate. I will focus on genuine structural concerns rather than minor implementation questions.

---

### Issue 1: Session-level random split violates temporal independence — the core claim is undermined by label leakage through user behavior

**The specific claim**: Stratified random sampling at the session level is presented as the split strategy. The rationale given is reducing variance from merchandising spikes.

**Why it is wrong**: The 7-day outcome window for conversion means that sessions from the same user can span a window where earlier sessions are in the test set and later sessions are in the training set, or vice versa. More critically, a user who converts in a session assigned to the test set may have sessions in the training set from the same 7-day window — sessions whose aggregate features (user_lifetime_purchases, user_return_rate) reflect behavior that occurred *after* the test session. This is a mild form of future leakage through user-level aggregates.

But there is a more fundamental problem: random session-level splits mean the model is evaluated on sessions drawn from the same calendar distribution as training data. In production, the model will always be used temporally out-of-sample — it will be applied to sessions occurring *after* the training period. A random split does not simulate this. A temporal split (e.g., first 120 days train, next 30 validation, final 30 test) would surface distribution shift from seasonal patterns, trend drift, and cohort effects. The random split will produce optimistic estimates of live performance, especially for the sequence model, which encodes item IDs — item ID embeddings trained on a randomly shuffled 180-day window will have seen representations of items that a temporally future model would have never encountered during training.

**What would constitute evidence**: Compare the log loss gap (sequence model vs. baseline) on a random split vs. a time-blocked split on the same data. If the gap shrinks substantially under temporal splitting, the random split is inflating the advantage of the sequence model's item ID embeddings. Alternatively, compute the fraction of item IDs in the test set that appear in the training set under both strategies; under temporal splitting this will be lower, and if sequence model performance degrades more than the baseline, the item ID signal is doing the work.

---

### Issue 2: Item ID embeddings are the dominant signal, not the sequence structure — the hypothesis mechanism is not isolated

**The specific claim**: The proposed model encodes "sequential" behavior (ordered event types, time deltas) and the ablation study (removing the sequence component) confirms sequence drives improvement.

**Why it is wrong**: The event representation concatenates item ID embedding, event type, and time delta. Item ID embeddings learned end-to-end will directly encode item-level conversion rates — a high-converting item will have an embedding that clusters with other high-converting items regardless of where in the sequence it appears. This means the model could achieve gains simply by memorizing item-level base rates through the embedding layer, with no actual use of sequence order.

The ablation described removes the sequence component but does not ablate item ID embeddings separately. A model with no sequence architecture but with item ID embeddings in the aggregate feature set could reproduce much of the gain. The claimed mechanism — that *ordering and timing of events* improves conversion prediction — is not what's being tested.

**What would constitute evidence**: Add a third condition: a non-sequential model (logistic regression or MLP) that receives item ID embeddings (pretrained or jointly trained) as aggregate features alongside the current aggregate features, but without sequence structure. If this model matches or approaches the sequence model's log loss, the gain is attributable to item ID memorization, not sequence modeling. A complementary test: train the sequence model with shuffled event order; if log loss is unchanged, sequence order is not being used.

---

### Issue 3: Log loss as primary metric is appropriate in principle but will be dominated by calibration of the majority class, obscuring whether the model is better *where it matters*

**The specific claim**: Log loss rewards appropriate probability mass assignment and penalizes overconfident mistakes, making it the right criterion for a downstream budgeting system at 2–5% positive rate.

**Why it is wrong**: At a 2–5% positive rate, the majority (95–98%) of log loss contribution comes from the negative class. A model that slightly adjusts probabilities on negatives — say, from 0.03 to 0.02 — achieves a large log loss improvement that has little business meaning. The sequence model, by encoding richer features, may find that adding any signal reduces calibration error on negatives while adding little information on positives. This produces a favorable log loss comparison that does not reflect whether the model is meaningfully better at identifying converters.

This is not just a metric choice concern — it directly affects model selection. The hyperparameter search optimizes log loss on the validation set, which means the sequence model's architecture and regularization will be tuned to minimize negative-class miscalibration, not to improve precision/recall or positive-class calibration.

**What would constitute evidence**: Report log loss decomposed into contributions from positives and negatives separately. A genuine improvement from the sequence model should show lower log loss on the positive class (the model is assigning higher probabilities to actual converters), not only on the negative class. If the improvement is entirely in negative log loss, the sequence is primarily refining "how certain are we this is not a conversion" rather than "how well can we identify conversions."

---

### Summary

The random split is the most structurally serious issue: it renders the evaluation optimistic in a way that is invisible from within the experiment and is especially favorable to the sequence model's item-embedding component. The item ID embedding confound undermines the core claim about sequential structure being the mechanism. The log loss decomposition concern is secondary but affects interpretation. The experiment as designed may produce a "correct" number on historical data that does not generalize and does not confirm the stated hypothesis mechanism.
all_issues_raised: Session-level random split across a 180-day window does not simulate temporal deployment conditions, producing optimistic estimates especially for item ID embeddings that benefit from memorizing in-window item statistics.

Item ID embeddings learned end-to-end can encode item-level base conversion rates without using sequence order, so the stated ablation does not isolate whether sequential structure or item memorization drives any improvement.

At 2–5% positive rate, log loss improvements can be dominated by negative-class calibration adjustments that have little business meaning; without decomposing log loss by class, model selection may optimize for the wrong signal.
all_issues_adjudicated: Session-level random split across a 180-day window does not simulate temporal deployment conditions, producing optimistic estimates especially for item ID embeddings that benefit from memorizing in-window item statistics.

Item ID embeddings learned end-to-end can encode item-level base conversion rates without using sequence order, so the stated ablation does not isolate whether sequential structure or item memorization drives any improvement.

At 2–5% positive rate, log loss improvements can be dominated by negative-class calibration adjustments that have little business meaning; without decomposing log loss by class, model selection may optimize for the wrong signal.
